question.text: What distinguishes a diagonal spread from vertical and horizontal (calendar) spreads?

answer.text: A diagonal spread combines options with different strike prices AND different expiry dates
A diagonal spread uses options of different types (one call and one put)
A diagonal spread uses options on different underlying assets
A diagonal spread combines a futures position with an options position

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Vertical spread: same expiry, different strikes. Horizontal (calendar) spread: same strike, different expiries. Diagonal spread: BOTH different strikes AND different expiries — it combines elements of both vertical and horizontal spreads. **The Trap:** A diagonal spread still uses the same option type (both calls or both puts) on the same underlying — it doesn't mix calls with puts or use different underlyings. **Cross-Exam Context:** All three series (I, IV, VIII Ch5) define these three spread types identically. The classification is universal across derivative markets. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Diagonal spreads are the most complex of the three — they profit from both directional moves and time decay differences simultaneously.
